name: Sun Quan
description: Sun Quan, also known as Zhongmou, was the founder of the Eastern Wu dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period of China. Born in 182 AD, he inherited control of the territories southeast of the Yangtze River after the death of his elder brother, Sun Ce, in 200 AD. Though initially under the shadow of his brother, Sun Quan quickly established himself as a capable ruler, balancing the ambitions of his advisors and generals while navigating the complex political landscape. He formed an alliance with Liu Bei to defeat Cao Cao at the Battle of Red Cliffs in 208 AD, a pivotal moment that prevented Cao Cao from unifying China and solidified the tripartite division. Later, he broke the alliance and seized Jing Province from Liu Bei, leading to the death of Guan Yu. In 229 AD, Sun Quan declared himself emperor, establishing the Eastern Wu dynasty and challenging the legitimacy of Cao Wei and Shu Han. His reign was marked by internal power struggles, military campaigns against Wei and Shu, and the development of the Jiangnan region. Sun Quan ruled for over 50 years, the longest of any of the Three Kingdoms rulers, leaving behind a legacy of strategic acumen, political maneuvering, and the establishment of a powerful southern kingdom that shaped the course of Chinese history. He is remembered as a shrewd and resilient leader who secured his family's legacy and contributed to the cultural and economic development of his realm.
subtitle: Founder of the Eastern Wu Dynasty
achievements: Secured territories southeast of the Yangtze
Formed alliance with Liu Bei against Cao Cao
Won the Battle of Red Cliffs

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Red Cliffs
keyMyths.description: Sun Quan's alliance with Liu Bei to defeat Cao Cao at Red Cliffs is a cornerstone of his legend. This victory secured the south and prevented Cao Cao's unification of China. It is detailed in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

keyMyths.title: Seizure of Jing Province
keyMyths.description: Sun Quan's betrayal of Liu Bei and subsequent seizure of Jing Province led to the death of Guan Yu. This marked a permanent split between Wu and Shu, escalating the Three Kingdoms conflict. Accounts vary across historical texts.

keyMyths.title: Becoming Emperor of Wu
keyMyths.description: Sun Quan's declaration as Emperor of Wu in 229 AD solidified his independence and challenged the legitimacy of Wei and Shu. This act formally established the Eastern Wu dynasty. This is recorded in the Records of the Three Kingdoms.

sources.author: Chen Shou

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
